_meta.headTerm: malignant carcinoid tumor of the duodenum

h1Title: Malignant Carcinoid Tumor of the Duodenum: Symptoms, Diagnosis & Treatment
heroOverview: Duodenal neuroendocrine tumors (NETs), historically called carcinoid tumors, arise from enterochromaffin cells of the duodenal mucosa and are the most common type of primary duodenal malignancy after adenocarcinoma. Population-based SEER data show duodenal NETs account for approximately 1-2% of all gastrointestinal malignancies, with an incidence of 0.4-0.5 per 100,000 per year and rising due to increased endoscopy. The vast majority are non-functioning, well-differentiated Grade 1 or 2 tumors presenting incidentally during upper endoscopy for dyspepsia or anaemia. However, duodenal NETs that arise near the ampulla of Vater can cause obstructive jaundice, and rare gastrin-producing tumors cause Zollinger-Ellison syndrome. Treatment ranges from endoscopic resection for small lesions to pancreaticoduodenectomy for advanced disease, with 5-year survival exceeding 90% for localized tumors and falling to 30-50% for those with regional or distant metastases.

keyStats.prevalence: 0.4-0.5 per 100,000 annually; represents 2-4% of all well-differentiated gastroenteropancreatic NETs; duodenum is the 3rd most common GI site after small bowel and rectum
keyStats.demographics: Slight male predominance (1.3:1); median age at diagnosis 60-65 years; rare under age 40
keyStats.avgAge: Median age 61 years; most diagnoses made between ages 55 and 75
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 1-2 cases per 100,000 annually worldwide; higher detection rates in regions with widespread endoscopy practice

definition: Malignant carcinoid tumor of the duodenum (ICD-10: C17.0, with behavior code /3 for malignant) is a well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor arising from serotonin-producing enterochromaffin cells in the duodenal mucosa. Duodenal NETs are classified by site (proximal duodenum versus periampullary), grade (WHO Grade 1: Ki-67 ≤2%, Grade 2: Ki-67 3-20%, Grade 3: Ki-67 >20%), and functionality (functioning with hormonal syndrome versus non-functioning). Most are non-functioning. The tumors progress through the same pathway as other foregut NETs: hyperplasia of enterochromaffin cells, microcarcinoidosis, and eventual invasive carcinoma. Duodenal NETs smaller than 10mm confined to the mucosa or submucosa have low metastatic potential (<5% for lesions <10mm); tumors with lymphovascular invasion, muscularis propria involvement, or size over 20mm have significantly higher metastatic risk. Histologically, they show characteristic organoid nesting, gyriform, or trabecular architecture with uniform cells expressing synaptophysin, chromogranin A, and CDX2. Prognosis is stage-dependent.

typesClassification.description: Asymptomatic or causes vague abdominal pain, dyspepsia, or anaemia from chronic bleeding; no hormonal syndrome. Discovered incidentally on endoscopy.
Rare; produces gastrin causing Zollinger-Ellison syndrome with severe peptic ulcer disease, diarrhoea, and hypergastrinemia. May be sporadic or part of MEN1.
Rare functioning tumor producing somatostatin; associated with neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) and typically presents with obstructive jaundice or pancreatitis.
Well-differentiated, low proliferative index; excellent prognosis with appropriate local treatment; most duodenal NETs are Grade 1.
Intermediate grade with higher recurrence risk; requires more extensive resection and closer follow-up.
Poorly differentiated high-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma; aggressive with high metastatic rate and poor prognosis; managed like small-cell lung cancer.

primarySymptoms: Non-specific upper abdominal discomfort or epigastric pain, often attributed to functional dyspepsia until imaging or endoscopy reveals the mass.
Chronic iron-deficiency anaemia from occult blood loss in non-functioning tumors, presenting with fatigue, pallor, and dyspnoea on exertion.
Obstructive jaundice with pruritus, dark urine, and pale stools when periampullary tumors compress or involve the distal common bile duct.
Early satiety, nausea, or vomiting from gastric outlet obstruction caused by large tumors in the first or second portion of the duodenum.
Diarrhoea and peptic ulcer disease refractory to standard therapy in gastrin-producing duodenal NETs (duodenal gastrinoma).
Palpable abdominal mass in advanced disease with bulky local extension.
Weight loss, anorexia, and fatigue in patients with advanced metastatic disease.
Flushing and secretory diarrhoea are uncommon in duodenal NETs (more typical of midgut carcinoids) but can occur with hepatic metastasis and serotonin release.
Back pain from invasion of the pancreas or retropancreatic structures in advanced periampullary tumors.
Gastrointestinal bleeding presenting as melaena or haematemesis from ulcerated duodenal lesions.
earlyWarningSigns: New-onset obstructive jaundice without gallstone disease or known pancreatic disease
Persistent iron-deficiency anaemia not explained by menstrual loss, colorectal pathology, or dietary factors
Peptic ulcer disease that is refractory to proton pump inhibitor therapy or recurs after treatment
Unexplained dyspepsia with epigastric mass on CT or MRI
Incidental duodenal subepithelial lesion found on upper endoscopy
Unexplained weight loss with normal appetite and no gastrointestinal infection
emergencySigns: Obstructive jaundice with fever and right upper quadrant pain suggesting cholangitis — requires urgent biliary drainage and antibiotics
Acute gastrointestinal haemorrhage with haematemesis or melaena and haemodynamic instability
Gastric outlet obstruction with severe vomiting, dehydration, and electrolyte disturbance
Acute pancreatitis from periampullary tumor obstruction of the pancreatic duct
Perforation of a duodenal ulcerated tumor with peritonitis

causes.cause: Sporadic genetic mutations in neuroendocrine cells
causes.description: Most duodenal NETs arise sporadically without a hereditary syndrome. Somatic mutations in DAXX, ATRX, MEN1, mTOR pathway genes (PTEN, TSC2), and TP53 accumulate and drive neoplastic transformation of enterochromaffin cells.

Approximately 5-10% of duodenal NETs, particularly gastrinomas, arise in the context of MEN1 from germline MEN1 gene mutations. These patients develop multifocal duodenal NETs and require lifetime surveillance.
NF1 is associated with somatostatin-rich duodenal NETs, typically in the periampullary region. Approximately 10-30% of duodenal somatostatinomas occur in NF1 patients.

Long-standing hypergastrinemia from chronic atrophic gastritis or proton pump inhibitor use causes gastrin cell hyperplasia in the gastric antrum and proximal duodenum, which can progress to gastrin-producing NETs.
Gastrin hypersecretion from a duodenal gastrinoma causes severe peptic ulcer disease, diarrhoea, and refractory heartburn. The tumour may be part of MEN1 or sporadic.

riskFactors.description: Incidence rises progressively after age 50; median age at diagnosis 61 years.
Slight male predominance (M:F ratio 1.3:1) across most population studies.
Germline MEN1 mutation confers high risk of multifocal duodenal gastrinomas; annual surveillance endoscopy and imaging recommended from age 20.
NF1 patients have elevated risk of periampullary somatostatinomas; surveillance recommended.
Long-term PPI use causes hypergastrinemia and gastric enterochromaffin-like cell hyperplasia; emerging evidence of association with gastric and duodenal NETs.
First-degree relatives of NET patients have a modestly elevated risk, suggesting possible inherited susceptibility.

affectedDemographics: Adults over 50, with median age at diagnosis of 61 years; rare before age 40.
Slight male predominance across all age groups.
Higher incidence reported in Western countries with widespread use of upper endoscopy.
Patients with hereditary syndromes (MEN1, NF1) present earlier (30-50 years) with multifocal disease.
No clear ethnic or geographic pattern identified; however, MEN1 has higher prevalence in certain populations.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis typically begins with upper endoscopy performed for dyspepsia, anaemia, or jaundice, which reveals a raised mucosal or submucosal lesion. Biopsy with forceps or jumbo forceps provides tissue for histology, Ki-67 grading, and IHC (synaptophysin, chromogranin A, CDX2, gastrin if gastrinoma suspected). Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) is the essential staging tool: it accurately measures tumor size (within 2mm), assesses depth of invasion (T stage), and evaluates locoregional lymph nodes with sensitivities of 80-90% for lesions >10mm. CT abdomen with contrast and MRI with diffusion weighting are used for staging metastatic disease. Ga-68 DOTATATE PET-CT is the most sensitive functional imaging for detecting somatostatin-receptor expressing NETs, with sensitivity >90% for well-differentiated tumors and is the standard for staging and surveillance. If gastrinoma is suspected, fasting serum gastrin and gastric acid pH are measured; a gastrin level above 10-fold normal with pH <2 confirms the diagnosis. Duodenal NETs must be distinguished from pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors, ampullary carcinoma, gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GIST), Brunner's gland hyperplasia, and metastatic disease.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Initial diagnosis; visualises the lesion and provides tissue for histology, grade (Ki-67), and IHC markers
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Sedation endoscopy, 15-20 minutes; biopsy taken with standard or jumbo forceps; results in 3-5 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Accurate local staging (size, depth, nodal involvement) and assessment for endoscopic resectability
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Sedation endoscopy with ultrasound probe; 20-30 minutes; fine-needle aspiration of suspicious nodes or pancreas if needed.

diagnosticTests.test: CT abdomen with contrast (portal venous phase)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Staging of liver metastases, nodal disease, and invasion of adjacent structures
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 20-minute scan; oral and IV contrast; results in 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Most sensitive functional imaging for well-differentiated NETs; detects occult metastases and somatostatin receptor expression for therapy planning
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Injection of radiolabelled somatostatin analogue; whole-body scan 60 minutes later; results in 1-2 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Fasting serum gastrin and gastric pH
diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnosis of gastrin-producing duodenal NET (duodenal gastrinoma); elevated gastrin with low gastric pH confirms
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood test after overnight fast; 24-hour fast may be required for ambiguous results.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Tumor marker for NET burden and response monitoring; elevated in most well-differentiated duodenal NETs
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood test; can be falsely elevated by PPI, renal failure, and other NETs; baseline and serial measurements.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Optimal for liver metastasis detection and characterization; superior to CT for hepatic lesions <10mm
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 45-minute scan; gadolinium contrast; no radiation; results in 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Endoscopic resection (EMR or ESD) for complete excision and staging
diagnosticTests.purpose: Both diagnostic and therapeutic; provides full pathology specimen for accurate T and N staging
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Therapeutic endoscopy under sedation; endoscopic mucosal resection (EMR) or endoscopic submucosal dissection (ESD); specimen retrieved for histology.

treatmentOverview: Management of duodenal NETs is stratified by stage, grade, size, and functional status. For localized tumors (<20mm, confined to mucosa/submucosa, Grade 1-2), endoscopic resection (EMR for lesions <10mm; ESD for larger or flat lesions) achieves complete resection in over 90% with excellent long-term outcomes. Ampullectomy by duodenoscopy is an alternative for small periampullary lesions. For tumors with adverse features (20-30mm, muscularis propria involvement, or lymph node positive), surgical resection with lymphadenectomy (pancreaticoduodenectomy for periampullary tumors or local resection with nodal dissection for distal duodenum) is indicated. Lymph node dissection is mandatory if nodes are involved. For metastatic disease (liver metastases, distant nodes), surgical resection of primary and metastases is considered only if complete debulking is feasible; otherwise, peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (PRRT with Lu-177 DOTATATE), somatostatin analogues (octreotide LAR, lanreotide), molecular targeted therapy (everolimus), and liver-directed therapies (TACE, SIRT, ablation) are used. For Grade 3 poorly differentiated tumors, platinum-based chemotherapy (capecitabine/temozolomide, FOLFOX) is the backbone. PRRT is the standard after failure of somatostatin analogues in SSTR+ Grade 1-2 metastatic disease, based on NETTER-1 trial data.

medicalTreatments.name: Octreotide LAR 20-30 mg intramuscularly every 4 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: Long-acting somatostatin analogue; first-line for symptomatic control of diarrhoea and flushing in functioning NETs and for tumor growth control in metastatic non-functioning disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom control in 60-70%; median PFS 14-16 months in pancreatic/GI NETs (CLARINET trial)

medicalTreatments.name: Lanreotide Autogel 120 mg subcutaneously every 4 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: Long-acting somatostatin analogue; similar efficacy to octreotide; preferred by some patients for ease of self-administration.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Tumor growth control and improved PFS in gastroenteropancreatic NETs; similar to octreotide LAR

medicalTreatments.name: Lu-177 DOTATATE PRRT (Peptide Receptor Radionuclide Therapy)
medicalTreatments.description: Radiolabelled somatostatin analogue delivering beta radiation to SSTR+ tumors; standard for metastatic well-differentiated NETs after somatostatin analogue failure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Median PFS 28 months vs 8 months for octreotide alone (NETTER-1 trial); ORR 18-30%; OS benefit demonstrated

medicalTreatments.name: Everolimus 10 mg daily orally
medicalTreatments.description: mTOR inhibitor; approved for progressive advanced pancreatic and GI NETs; targets the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: PFS benefit of 11 months vs 4 months for placebo in RADIANT-4; ORR 10-15%; stable disease in 70%

medicalTreatments.name: Capecitabine + temozolomide (CAPTEM)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral chemotherapy for Grade 2-3 well-differentiated NETs and pancreatic NETs; works synergistically through fluoropyrimidine and alkylator mechanisms.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ORR 30-40% in pancreatic NETs; 15-20% in GI NETs; useful for rapidly progressive or Grade 3 disease

medicalTreatments.name: Sunitinib 37.5 mg daily
medicalTreatments.description: Multi-targeted TKI; approved for progressive pancreatic NETs; targets VEGFR and PDGFR for anti-angiogenic effect.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Median PFS 11.4 months vs 5.5 months for placebo; ORR 10%; mainly used for pancreatic NETs

medicalTreatments.name: Liver-directed therapy (TACE, SIRT, ablation)
medicalTreatments.description: Transarterial chemoembolization (TACE), selective internal radiation therapy (Y-90 SIRT), or percutaneous thermal ablation for liver-dominant metastatic disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Objective response rates 40-60%; symptom control for hepatic tumor burden; used as bridging to systemic therapy

medicalTreatments.name: Platinum-based chemotherapy (cisplatin/carboplatin + etoposide) for Grade 3
medicalTreatments.description: Standard chemotherapy for poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinoma (Grade 3 Ki-67 >20%); similar approach to small-cell lung cancer.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ORR 30-50% in poorly differentiated disease; short duration of response (6-9 months); palliation of symptoms

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic mucosal resection (EMR) — for lesions <10mm, confined to mucosa
surgicalOptions.description: Standard polypectomy technique or cap EMR for small superficial duodenal NETs; complete en-bloc resection for accurate pathology staging.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Complete resection >90% for lesions <10mm; recurrence <5% with negative margins

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic submucosal dissection (ESD) — for lesions 10-20mm or flat lesions
surgicalOptions.description: En-bloc resection of larger or deeper duodenal lesions with adequate margins; higher technical demand but superior to EMR for accurate pathology.
surgicalOptions.successRate: En-bloc resection 85-95%; R0 resection 80-90%; lower recurrence than EMR for larger lesions

surgicalOptions.name: Duodenal resection with lymphadenectomy — for tumors 20-30mm or node-positive
surgicalOptions.description: Transduodenal or segmental duodenal resection with nodal dissection; organ-sparing where feasible.
surgicalOptions.successRate: R0 resection in 80-90%; lymph node control 70-80%; recurrence rates <10% for localized disease

surgicalOptions.name: Pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) — for periampullary NETs >30mm or node-positive periampullary tumors
surgicalOptions.description: Radical resection of the pancreatic head, duodenum, and regional lymph nodes for advanced periampullary disease.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year survival 60-80% for node-negative; 40-60% for node-positive; operative mortality 2-5% at high-volume centres

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical debulking of primary and liver metastases — for metastatic disease with high tumor burden
surgicalOptions.description: Cytoreductive surgery to remove primary and bulky hepatic disease when complete resection is achievable; improves symptoms and survival.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptom improvement in 80%; objective response in 60%; improved OS when complete cytoreduction achieved

alternativeTreatments.name: Surveillance for small (<10mm) incidental non-functioning duodenal NETs
alternativeTreatments.description: Active monitoring with annual endoscopy and EUS for low-risk lesions where risks of intervention outweigh benefits. Appropriate for elderly or comorbid patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Selective internal radiation therapy (Y-90 SIRT)
alternativeTreatments.description: Liver-directed radiation for hepatic metastases when TACE is contraindicated or has failed; delivers high-dose radiation to tumor while sparing normal liver.

alternativeTreatments.name: Radiofrequency ablation or microwave ablation of liver metastases
alternativeTreatments.description: Percutaneous or intraoperative thermal ablation for limited hepatic metastatic disease (<5 lesions, each <50mm).

whySeeSpecialist: Duodenal NET management requires a multidisciplinary team: gastroenterologist for endoscopy and EUS, hepatobiliary/pancreatic surgeon for surgical resection, medical oncologist and nuclear medicine physician for systemic therapy and PRRT, and endocrinologist for hormone-related complications. Referral to a NET specialist centre is recommended for all cases to ensure accurate grading, staging, and access to all treatment modalities.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a NET specialist centre with high-volume endoscopy, on-site pancreatic surgery, PRRT capability, and a multidisciplinary tumor board. Ask about their experience with duodenal NETs specifically, their endoscopic resection volume, and their approach to small versus large periampullary tumors.
hospitalCriteria: Multidisciplinary NET specialist centre with weekly tumor board
High-volume therapeutic endoscopy with EUS and ESD capability
Pancreatic and hepatobiliary surgical programme with Whipple procedure expertise
Nuclear medicine department with Ga-68 PET-CT and Lu-177 PRRT
Medical oncology with NET chemotherapy, everolimus, and clinical trial access
keyFacilities: NET specialist centres with dedicated NET nurses and coordinators
Advanced endoscopy with EUS, EMR, and ESD
preventionStrategies: No specific preventive measures exist for sporadic duodenal NETs.
Surveillance endoscopy every 1-2 years in MEN1 patients from age 20 to detect small duodenal NETs before they grow or metastasise.
Annual MRI or CT surveillance in patients with known duodenal NET after resection.
Chromogranin A monitoring every 3-6 months in metastatic disease to detect progression early.
Ga-68 DOTATATE PET-CT surveillance every 12-18 months for metastatic well-differentiated NETs on treatment.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain a balanced diet with adequate protein and fibre; small frequent meals if diarrhoea or dumping syndrome is present.
Avoid alcohol or limit to moderate intake, particularly if liver metastases or treatment-related liver toxicity is present.
Engage in light regular exercise; fatigue is common but activity supports recovery and wellbeing.
Seek psychological support for anxiety related to NET diagnosis, particularly given the chronic nature of metastatic disease.
Avoid excessive sun exposure if on everolimus (photosensitivity risk).

recommended: High-protein diet (1.0-1.2 g/kg/day) to maintain muscle mass during treatment
Small, frequent meals to manage diarrhoea and early satiety
Complex carbohydrates with soluble fibre to slow gastric emptying and improve diarrhoea control
Electrolyte replacement (sodium, potassium, magnesium) if diarrhoea is persistent
avoid: Large high-fat meals which may exacerbate diarrhoea and dumping syndrome
Excessive alcohol (hepatotoxicity risk with everolimus, liver metastases)
Raw or undercooked foods during periods of immunosuppression (post-chemotherapy)
Grapefruit juice with everolimus (CYP3A4 interaction raising drug levels)

exerciseGuidelines: Regular light-to-moderate activity (walking, cycling, yoga) is encouraged. No restrictions unless specific symptoms (jaundice, severe fatigue, liver dysfunction) limit capacity. Listen to your body and rest when needed, especially after chemotherapy cycles.
dailyManagement: Take somatostatin analogue injections on schedule every 4 weeks; do not delay or skip doses.
Report new abdominal pain, jaundice, melaena, or change in bowel habit to the oncology team promptly.
Take everolimus at the same time each day with or without food; avoid grapefruit juice.
Monitor weight weekly; unexplained weight loss may signal progression.
Keep a symptom diary to track diarrhoea frequency, flushing episodes, and appetite changes for review at clinic visits.
prognosis: Duodenal NETs have an excellent prognosis when diagnosed early and treated with curative-intent resection. Five-year survival exceeds 90% for Stage I-II disease resected with negative margins. For node-positive localized disease, 5-year survival is 60-80% with appropriate surgery. Metastatic disease has a median overall survival of 28-40 months with modern therapy; long-term survival of 5-10 years is achievable in patients with indolent Grade 1 metastatic disease and good treatment response. Poor prognostic factors include: high grade (Grade 3), large tumor size (>30mm), periampullary location, hepatic metastases, and Ki-67 >10%. Gastrinomas with MEN1 have a chronic relapsing course but are rarely the cause of death.
recoveryTimeline: After endoscopic resection, patients are discharged the same day or after overnight observation with minimal restrictions. Recovery of the duodenal mucosa takes 2-4 weeks. After pancreaticoduodenectomy, hospital stay is typically 10-14 days with full recovery in 6-8 weeks. Post-PRRT, fatigue is common for 1-2 weeks. Liver-directed therapy recovery is 1-2 weeks. Patients on long-acting somatostatin analogues require ongoing 4-weekly injections indefinitely.
complications: Obstructive jaundice and cholangitis from periampullary tumor involvement — managed with biliary stenting or surgical bypass.
Upper gastrointestinal haemorrhage from ulcerated duodenal tumor — endoscopic haemostasis, radiation, or surgery.
Gastric outlet obstruction from bulky duodenal mass — endoscopic stenting or surgical bypass.
Liver metastases with hepatic replacement causing pain, cholestasis, and hepatic failure — managed with liver-directed therapy and systemic options.
Metastatic progression to peritoneum with ascites and bowel obstruction — palliative paracentesis and systemic therapy.
Carcinoid heart disease with right-sided valvular thickening and regurgitation from chronic serotonin excess — rare in duodenal NETs but requires echocardiography surveillance in metastatic disease.
Chemotherapy or everolimus toxicity including myelosuppression, stomatitis, and metabolic effects — dose modification and supportive care.
PRRT-related bone marrow suppression and nephrotoxicity — requires monitoring of blood counts and renal function.

supportResources.name: Neuroendocrine Tumor Research Foundation (NETRF)
supportResources.description: US patient organisation funding NET research, providing education, and connecting patients with specialists and clinical trials.

supportResources.name: Northern California Carcinoid / NET Cancer Foundation (the LACUNAs)
supportResources.description: Patient advocacy organization providing information, support groups, and conference education for carcinoid/NET patients.

supportResources.name: European Neuroendocrine Tumor Society (ENETS)
supportResources.description: Professional society providing evidence-based guidelines, centre accreditation, and patient information on NETs.

supportResources.name: Cancer Research UK — neuroendocrine tumours
supportResources.description: UK patient information on NETs including duodenal carcinoids, with treatment overviews and support resources.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pancreatic Neuroendocrine Tumor
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pancreatic NETs (pNETs) arise in the pancreas and may be functional (insulinoma, gastrinoma) or non-functioning. Duodenal NETs arise in the duodenal wall; the distinction matters for surgical approach. EUS and CT typically distinguish the two anatomically.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gastrointestinal Stromal Tumor (GIST)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: GIST is a sarcoma of the GI tract with KIT/CD117 expression. Duodenal NET is a neuroendocrine tumor expressing synaptophysin and chromogranin. Immunohistochemistry and histology establish the diagnosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ampullary Carcinoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ampullary carcinoma arises from the ampulla of Vater epithelium and presents with obstructive jaundice. Duodenal NET arises from the duodenal wall near the ampulla; histology (NET markers vs adenocarcinoma markers) distinguishes them.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gastrinoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Gastrinoma is a functional NET producing gastrin. Duodenal gastrinoma is a specific duodenal NET causing Zollinger-Ellison syndrome. Other duodenal NETs are non-functioning and have no gastrin syndrome.

relatedConditions.name: Pancreatic Neuroendocrine Tumor
relatedConditions.relevance: Pancreatic NETs and duodenal NETs share similar classification, treatment approaches, and monitoring strategies. They may co-occur in MEN1 syndrome.

relatedConditions.name: Gastrointestinal Neuroendocrine Tumor
relatedConditions.relevance: Duodenal NET is a subset of GI NETs; the overall management framework applies, but duodenal location influences surgical approach.

relatedConditions.name: Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Duodenal gastrinoma is the most common cause of Zollinger-Ellison syndrome. Recognition of the gastrinoma-diagnosis link is critical for appropriate management of refractory ulcer disease.

faqs.question: What is a duodenal carcinoid tumor?
faqs.answer: A duodenal carcinoid tumor (now called duodenal neuroendocrine tumor or NET) is a rare malignancy arising from hormone-producing cells in the duodenal wall. Most are non-functioning and discovered incidentally during endoscopy.

faqs.question: Is a duodenal carcinoid tumor cancer?
faqs.answer: Yes. Duodenal carcinoid tumors are malignant neuroendocrine cancers, classified by behaviour code /3 in ICD-10. However, they are often slow-growing with excellent 5-year survival when caught early.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of a duodenal carcinoid tumor?
faqs.answer: Most early tumors cause no symptoms. When present, symptoms include non-specific abdominal pain, iron-deficiency anaemia from bleeding, and obstructive jaundice if near the ampulla. Advanced tumors may cause nausea, vomiting, weight loss, or a palpable mass.

faqs.question: How is a duodenal carcinoid diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Upper endoscopy with biopsy is the initial diagnostic test. Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) provides accurate local staging (size, depth, lymph nodes). Ga-68 DOTATATE PET-CT is the most sensitive test for detecting metastases.

faqs.question: What is the difference between carcinoid and neuroendocrine tumor?
faqs.answer: Carcinoid is an older term for well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumors. WHO now uses the term 'well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor' (NET) for what were historically called carcinoids, to reflect their endocrine nature more accurately.

faqs.question: Can duodenal carcinoid tumors spread?
faqs.answer: Yes. Small tumors (<10mm) confined to the mucosa have <5% metastatic risk. Larger tumors or those with lymphovascular invasion have up to 40-60% risk of spread to regional lymph nodes or liver.

faqs.question: How is a small duodenal carcinoid treated?
faqs.answer: Small tumors (<10mm, confined to the mucosa) are treated with endoscopic resection (EMR or ESD), which removes the lesion completely without surgery. Regular follow-up endoscopy is required.

faqs.question: When is surgery needed for a duodenal carcinoid?
faqs.answer: Surgery is needed for tumors >20mm, tumors with muscularis propria invasion or lymph node involvement, or periampullary tumors >30mm. Options include duodenal resection, ampullectomy, or pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple) for advanced periampullary disease.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis for duodenal carcinoid?
faqs.answer: Five-year survival exceeds 90% for early-stage tumors treated with complete endoscopic or surgical resection. Metastatic disease has median survival of 28-40 months with modern therapy; indolent Grade 1 disease can survive 5-10 years.

faqs.question: What is a gastrinoma of the duodenum?
faqs.answer: A duodenal gastrinoma is a rare functioning NET producing gastrin, causing Zollinger-Ellison syndrome with severe peptic ulcer disease, diarrhoea, and hypergastrinemia. It may be sporadic or part of MEN1.

faqs.question: What is a somatostatinoma of the duodenum?
faqs.answer: A somatostatinoma is a rare functioning duodenal NET that produces somatostatin, causing mild diabetes, diarrhoea, steatorrhoea, and gallbladder disease. About 30% are associated with NF1.

faqs.question: Are duodenal carcinoids hereditary?
faqs.answer: Most are sporadic. However, 5-10% occur in MEN1 (multifocal duodenal NETs, particularly gastrinomas) and some in NF1 (somatostatinomas). Family screening is not routinely recommended for sporadic cases.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for metastatic duodenal carcinoid?
faqs.answer: Metastatic disease is managed with somatostatin analogues (octreotide, lanreotide), peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (PRRT with Lu-177 DOTATATE), everolimus, capecitabine/temozolomide chemotherapy, and liver-directed therapies.

faqs.question: What is PRRT for carcinoid tumors?
faqs.answer: PRRT (peptide receptor radionuclide therapy) is a targeted radiation treatment using a radiolabelled somatostatin analogue (Lu-177 DOTATATE) that binds to somatostatin receptors on NET cells and delivers therapeutic radiation. It is standard after somatostatin analogue failure in metastatic well-differentiated NETs.

faqs.question: Does a duodenal carcinoid cause carcinoid syndrome?
faqs.answer: Carcinoid syndrome (flushing, diarrhoea, wheezing) is rare with duodenal NETs because the portal circulation metabolises serotonin before it reaches the systemic circulation. It occurs mainly with liver metastases or primary tumors draining directly into systemic circulation.

faqs.question: How often should follow-up happen after treatment?
faqs.answer: After endoscopic resection, endoscopy with EUS at 6 months then annually for 3 years. After surgical resection, CT or MRI at 6, 12, 24 months. For metastatic disease on treatment, chromogranin A every 3 months and imaging every 6-12 months.

faqs.question: Can duodenal carcinoid tumors come back?
faqs.answer: Yes. Local recurrence after endoscopic resection occurs in <5% with negative margins. Lymph node or liver recurrence occurs in 10-20% of node-positive or large tumors. Long-term surveillance with imaging is essential.

faqs.question: What is the risk of MEN1 for family members?
faqs.answer: If the duodenal NET is associated with MEN1 (gastrinoma with family history or other endocrine tumors), first-degree relatives should undergo MEN1 genetic testing and annual biochemical and imaging surveillance.

faqs.question: Can I take a normal diet with a duodenal carcinoid?
faqs.answer: Yes, unless specific symptoms (jaundice, diarrhoea, dumping) require dietary modification. A balanced diet with adequate protein is recommended. Avoid grapefruit with everolimus and limit alcohol.

faqs.question: Is there a blood test for duodenal carcinoid?
faqs.answer: Chromogranin A (CgA) is the main blood tumor marker for NETs, elevated in most duodenal NETs. It is used for monitoring rather than diagnosis. Gastrin level is tested specifically when gastrinoma is suspected.

faqs.question: How does obesity affect duodenal carcinoid prognosis?
faqs.answer: Obesity is not a direct risk factor for duodenal NETs. However, it complicates surgical approaches and may affect overall health and treatment tolerability. Maintaining healthy weight through balanced diet and activity is beneficial.

simpleName: Malignant carcinoid tumor of the duodenum
metaTitle: Duodenal Carcinoid Tumor: Symptoms, Diagnosis & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Malignant duodenal carcinoid (duodenal NET): symptoms, endoscopic treatment, surgery, PRRT, and prognosis. Medically reviewed guide.
